summary: Gruber reports on Apple's App Store censorship of Ninjawords, an iPhone dictionary app that was forced to remove common English words deemed 'objectionable' by Apple reviewers and slapped with a 17+ rating even after censorship. The approval process took two months, with multiple rejections because reviewers deliberately searched for profanity in a dictionary. Gruber draws comparisons to Amazon's Kindle (which ships with a full dictionary) and even Walmart, arguing Apple's censorship is more extreme than both. The post details the Kafkaesque back-and-forth between the developers and Apple's review team, including being sent to the back of the queue for a metadata change. Gruber frames this as the most outrageous App Store rejection yet, highlighting the absurdity of censoring a reference book.
key_points: Apple forced Ninjawords to remove common English words like 'ass', 'cock', and 'screw' from a dictionary app, despite these having non-objectionable meanings
Even after censoring the dictionary, Apple required a 17+ age rating — more restrictive than elementary school libraries that stock physical dictionaries with the same words
The approval process took two months with multiple rejections, and Apple refused to expedite even a simple metadata change, forcing a full binary resubmission
Apple's censorship standards are more extreme than Walmart's, which sells uncensored dictionaries without warning labels
App Store reviewers actively searched for profanity they already knew, rather than encountering it through normal use
money_quotes: A dictionary. A reference book. For words contained in all reasonable dictionaries. For words contained in dictionaries that are used every day in elementary school libraries and classrooms.
Apple's App Store review team makes Walmart seem liberal by comparison.
In other words, the App Store reviewer(s) explicitly searched for curse words they already knew, and found them.
Not only must the dictionary be censored — a dictionary — but even after being purged of 'objectionable' words it would only be considered with a 17+ rating.
Every time I think I've seen the most outrageous App Store rejection, I'm soon proven wrong.
Apple requires you to be 17 years or older to purchase a censored dictionary that omits half the words Steve Jobs uses every day.
key_insight: Apple's App Store review process had become so absurdly censorious that it was forcing developers to bowdlerize English dictionaries — reference works freely available in elementary schools — and then restricting the censored result to adults only.
